coredata.dc:title: Preparation and performance of self-cleaning synergistic visible light catalytic coatings based on N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 for NO degradation

coredata.dc:description: Due to the intensification of nitric oxide (NO) emissions caused by industrial development, visible light responsive photocatalysts have been selected and applied in building coatings as a new strategy. In this study, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)-Bi2WO6 (NBW) was prepared using an ethylene glycol-assisted solvothermal method. Various techniques, including XRD, FT-IR, SEM, were employed to analyze and characterize NBW. The photocatalytic performance and stability of NBW were evaluated, and the results showed that 52% of NO (Initial concentration: 600 ppb) were oxidized under visible light. NBW was dispersed in an ethyl acetate/polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)/polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) suspension and modified by 1H,1H,2H,2H-Perfluorodecyltriethoxysilane (PFDTES), to form an easy-to-spray coating referred to as CPFB. Due to the photocatalytic ability of NBW, CPFB oxidized 74% of NO under visible light and reduced the coloring of coating surfaces contaminated with methyl red. The superhydrophobicity of CPFB coating provided the foundation for its self-cleaning ability. In addition, CPFB exhibited hydrophobicity and photocatalytic performance even after tests such as photooxidation, water impact, and wear. Finally, the photocatalytic mechanism of CPFB self-cleaning coating was explored. The significance of CPFB lied in its ability to effectively combine self-cleaning, visible light-responsive photocatalysis, and durability in a single coating. This innovative approach may inspire the development of similar coatings for various applications.

XRD patterns (a), FT-IR spectra (b), survey spectra (c) of NBW and high resolution XPS spectra in Bi 4f (d), W 4f (e), C 1s (f), N 1s (g) and O 1s (h).
SEM images of NBW precursor (a–b), the element distribution of Bi, W, O, C, and N in NBW (c), EDX spectra of NBW sample in different areas (d–e), TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of NBW (f–g).

UV–Vis absorption spectra (a), band gaps (b), PL emission spectra (c), transient photocurrent response (d), EIS Nyquist plots (e), and the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution curves (inset) of NBW.

Visible or full spectrum PCD curves of NO of different NBW (a–b), PCD performances of NBW with different scavengers(c), PCD performance of NBW synthesized in different solvents (d), different hydrothermal temperatures (e), and hydrothermal time (f).
Recyclability of NBW for oxidation under visible light (a) and full Spectrum (b), the FT-IR spectra(c), and XRD patterns(d) of NBW before and after cycling runs.

FT-IR spectra of NBW and F-NBW (a), UV–Vis absorption spectrum of ethyl acetate solution (b, inset was the digital photo of ethyl acetate solution), UV–Vis absorption spectra(c) and TGA and DTG curves (d) of CPFB powder, SEM images of CPFB (e–f).

Visible/full spectrum PCD curves of NO in the presence of coatings with different concentrations of NBW (a–b), PCD performances of CPFB-2 with different scavengers(c), the results of methyl red discoloration test for CPFB (d, illustrated with surface photos after different light irradiation time and the PCD curves of CPFB on NO after methyl red degradation), dependence of ln(C/C0) vs. irradiation time. The self-cleaning performance of CPFB-2 on different pollutants (a–b), and the liquid repellency of CPFB-2 on dye aqueous solutions (c–d).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the intensification of nitric oxide (NO) emissions caused by industrial development, visible light responsive photocatalysts have been selected and applied in building coatings as a new strategy. In this study, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)-Bi2WO6 (NBW) was prepared using an ethylene glycol-assisted solvothermal method. Various techniques, including XRD, FT-IR, SEM, were employed to analyze and characterize NBW. The photocatalytic performance and stability of NBW were evaluated, and the results showed that 52% of NO (Initial concentration: 600 ppb) were oxidized under visible light. NBW was dispersed in an ethyl acetate/polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)/polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) suspension and modified by 1H,1H,2H,2H-Perfluorodecyltriethoxysilane (PFDTES), to form an easy-to-spray coating referred to as CPFB. Due to the photocatalytic ability of NBW, CPFB oxidized 74% of NO under visible light and reduced the coloring of coating surfaces contaminated with methyl red. The superhydrophobicity of CPFB coating provided the foundation for its self-cleaning ability. In addition, CPFB exhibited hydrophobicity and photocatalytic performance even after tests such as photooxidation, water impact, and wear. Finally, the photocatalytic mechanism of CPFB self-cleaning coating was explored. The significance of CPFB lied in its ability to effectively combine self-cleaning, visible light-responsive photocatalysis, and durability in a single coating. This innovative approach may inspire the development of similar coatings for various applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid industrial and automotive growth, the emission of nitric oxide (NO) from the combustion of fossil fuels has become a significant environmental and health concern (Cai et al., 2023). NO not only easily generates acid rain and photochemical smog in the environment, but also poses health risks to human bodies (Yu et al., 2020). Recent advancements in green and effective photocatalytic degradation (PCD) have provided a promising solution for NO removal (Lu et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022a).

Among PCD materials that respond to visible light, Bi2WO6 (BW) has attracted the attention due to its small band gap, high chemical stability, and non-toxic characteristics (Wang et al., 2021). However, the high electron-hole recombination rates, limited visible light absorption ability, and weak electron migration ability of BW require further improvement (Jing et al., 2021). Traditional methods for improvements include morphology control, ion doping, and heterojunction construction (Zhu et al., 2020). Lu et al. enhanced the photocatalytic activity of BW by inserting I into BW nanosheets, causing 50% NO to be oxidized (Lu et al., 2021). However, it is difficult for large-sized materials to form a perfect interface with BW, generating new electron-hole recombination centers at the interface. Thus, the use of small-sized materials such as quantum dots not only improved the light absorption ability of NBW, but also acted as electron acceptors and electron transfer centers, prolonging the lifetime of photo generated carriers (Liu et al., 2021b). To further improve the ability to absorb light and photoluminescence characteristics of PCD materials, it is also advisable to modify quantum dots as necessary (Liu et al., 2020a). Zhang et al. obtained BW with good visible light response by modifying it with N-doped CQDs, which effectively degraded organic pollutants under visible light (Zhang et al., 2018). At present, research on the NO removal of CQDs/BW and its further enhancement was limited, and there was a lack of specific attempts to apply such photocatalysts.

Numerous studies have shown the dramatic impact of the Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect on improving electron transfer efficiency, increasing hole electron lifetime, and improving photocatalytic activity (Wang et al., 2016). Under specific conditions, Bi metal was deposited on the surface of BW using ethylene glycol as a reducing agent, to meet the SPR conditions. Hua and Zhang et al. used ethylene glycol-assisted solvothermal method to load Bi metal onto BW microspheres which was the main reason for the increased visible light catalytic activity of BW (Hua et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019). Meanwhile, ethylene glycol as an auxiliary solvent seemed to help introduce a large number of oxygen vacancies on BW materials that have a positive impact on the ability to remove NO (Huo et al., 2019). Unfortunately, it was still difficult to overcome the limitations caused by catalyst powder in practical applications of this type of catalyst.

Photocatalytic coatings, known for their cost-effectiveness, high efficiency, and versatility, were widely used in functional building materials (Xia et al., 2019a). The effective degradation of NO by photocatalytic coatings had also been confirmed by many researchers (Liu et al., 2022b). Among them, TiO2, as a traditional PCD material, was widely used in photocatalytic coatings (Liu et al., 2022c). However, its large bandgap energy reduced its absorption intensity of visible light, greatly limiting its applications (Fang et al., 2022). In fact, PCD materials which were driven by visible light, such as BW, had begun to be applied in the fields of photocatalytic fabrics and marine anti-fouling, with significant self-cleaning and antibacterial properties. The Bi2WO6/boron grafted polyurethane composite coating prepared by Wang had significant antibacterial and resistance adhesion properties (Wang et al., 2022). Song prepared RGO/Bi2WO6 material and combined it with fluorocarbon resin, resulting in a composite coating with a bactericidal rate of over 93% under visible light (Song et al., 2021).

Nonetheless, there had been limited research on BW as a PCD coating component specifically for NO degradation. In addition, long-term use of the coating leaded to the accumulation of deposits on the coating surface and blocking the active sites. More importantly, relying solely on the photocatalytic properties of PCD materials did not guarantee long-term self-cleaning ability (Leng and Yu, 2016). To address this limitation, the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and superhydrophobicity had been proposed by researchers as an effective self-cleaning strategy. This route not only chemically degraded substances on the coating surface but also prevented the physical adsorption of substances such as dust (Liu et al., 2021c).

Therefore, the introduction of superhydrophobic characteristics into the coating becomed essential. Furthermore, systematic research on the application of PCD materials such as BW in self-cleaning coatings was also particularly important to optimal performance (Xu et al., 2023). As the foundation for the surface roughness structure of superhydrophobic coatings, PCD materials required the use of some low surface energy material (Deng et al., 2022). Moreover, it was critical to select reasonable auxiliary film-forming materials to prevent damages from photo oxidation caused by pure PCD materials (Liu et al., 2020b). Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was a promising substrate for photocatalytic superhydrophobic coatings, with resistance to photo oxidation damage of coatings and assistance in reducing surface energy. Combined with PCD materials, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) provided rougher surface structures (Wang et al., 2013).

This study presented a simple method to prepare N-CQDs doped BW (NBW) with Bi metal, in order to achieve effective NO removal. PFDTES modified NBW improved its dispersion in PTFE/PVDF film forming materials. The combination of PTFE and NBW formed a rough surface structure, while PVDF provided low surface energy substances. The prepared CPFB coating demonstrated excellent anti-fouling, self-cleaning, and photocatalytic activity. After experiencing harsh conditions such as cyclic NO oxidation, water shock, photo oxidation, and acid-base salt corrosion, the coating showed stability. CPFB coatings may have broad applications in tunnel inner wall and building outer wall coatings.

Ammonium citrate was purchased from Lijie Chemical Co., Ltd (Zhejiang, China). Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were purchased from Haohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), 1H,1H,2H,2H- Perfluorodecyltriethoxysilane (PFDTES, 96%) and Na2WO4·2H2O were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and ethylenediamine were provided by Shandong Huaxia Shenzhou New Material Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol, ammonia, and ethanol were purchased from Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytically pure. The deionized water and anhydrous ethanol (99 %) were also used throughout the experiment.

10 mmoL of ammonium citrate and 670 μL ethylenediamine were added to 20 mL of deionized water. The uniformly stirred solution was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h. After the large particles in the reaction solution were removed by centrifugation, the supernatant was labeled as N-CQDs.

1 mmol Bi(NO3)3‧5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of glycol solution. Meanwhile, 0.5 mmoL Na2WO4‧2H2O was dissolved in a certain concentration of 20 mL N-CQDs aqueous solution, which was slowly added to ethylene glycol solution to form a suspension and stirred for 2 h, which was then transferred to a 50 mL autoclave at 180 °C for 20 h.

N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 was recorded based on the ratio of the volume of N-CQDs to the amount of Bi(NO3)3‧5H2O substance (Table 1 ). The prepared samples were denoted as BW, NBW-0.5, NBW-1, NBW-2. For comparison, catalysts synthesized in solvent systems with different water/ethylene glycol volume ratios were prepared and named according to the volume ratio of water/system solvent in the solvent system, such as NBW-1-0, NBW-1-50, and NBW-1-100.

After the substrate was polished, a self-cleaning coating was obtained through a simple spraying method. 0.4 g of PTFE was uniformly dispersed in a 25 mL mixture of PVDF/ethyl acetate which dissolved 0.6 g PVDF. Quantitative NBW, 50 μl TEOS, and 250 μl PFDTES were sequentially added to the system and stirred for 2 h to form a PVDF/PTFE/NBW suspension, which was then sprayed at a pressure of 20–30 psi per square inch and a spraying distance of 15 cm. The name of the NBW self-cleaning coating was determined by the relative mass of NBW in the coating board, such as CPF, CPFB-0.5, CPFB-1, CPFB-1.5, CPFB-2, CPFB-3.

The morphology of the sample was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) with a test voltage of 15.0 kV and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, America). The element mapping images of the CPFB-2 coating were obtained on an energy dispersive spectroscope (EDS, HORIBA). Fourier-transform infrared spectra were obtained using a Bruker Tensor-II FT-IR spectrometer (Tensor27, Bruker Germany). The chemical bonding state of samples was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA) with a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source (KE = 1486.6 eV, 150 W). All XPS spectra were corrected by the C1s peak of external hydrocarbon contamination located at 284.8 eV. Thermal stability was analyzed with thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) by Discovery synchronous thermal analyzer (SDT 650). The specific surface area was detected by BET (Geminin VII 2390, Micromeritics, America).

The reaction of NO oxidation was carried out in a 2.5 L stainless steel rectangular reactor with an upper surface made of quartz glass, which was maintained at 25 °C. A long arc xenon lamp (500 W) with a cut-off filter（λ > 420 nm) was used as the light source，the full spectrum light was obtained from a xenon lamp without a filter attached. The photocatalyst powder (0.1 g) was evenly distributed on the bottom of the reactor, or the photocatalyst coating test plate was placed in the reactor. The NO gas comes from a compressed gas bottle, in which the NO content is 101 ppm (diluted with N2). The initial NO concentration was diluted to about 600 ppb by using the airflow generated by the zero-air generator (Teledyne Technologies, Model 701). When the gas system reached the adsorption-desorption balance, the lamp was turned on. A chemiluminescence NOx analyzer (Teledyne technology, T200) was used to continuously monitor the NO concentration at 0.7 L min−1.

The contact angle (CA) of water, ethylene glycol, and glycerol was tested by Guangdong Daint contact angle measuring instrument JC2000D, which Indicated the wetting state of different polar liquids on the CPFB coating. A simple method was used to estimate the sliding angle of water droplets on the CPFB: the length of the CPFB template was recorded as L, and when the CPFB template carrying 2 μL of water droplets was gently lifted to a certain height (h), the droplets just rolled. The rolling angle was estimated as arcsin (h/L).

A 500 W xenon lamp was used to simulate the accelerated aging process of the coating at a distance of 40 cm above the surface. Rinsing the coating at a pressure of 60 psi at a distance of 30 cm from the coating was used to simulate the process of coating being rinsed during daily use. The wear resistance provided by a 100 g weight and 800 grit sandpaper further tested the durability of the coating. The coating was soaked in a solution of 10% H2SO4, NaOH, and NaCl to test the corrosion resistance of the coating. Compare the CA and NO degradation ability of fresh coatings with boards that have undergone the above tests to test the durability of the coatings.

Various powders and organic dyes with different particle sizes were selected to simulate pollutants. Pollutants with different distribution states were washed with water, and the state of pollutants on the coating surface was detected before and after washing. In addition, the coating was vertically inserted into the aqueous solution dyed with organic dyes and taken out to test the stain resistance of the coating surface by detecting the change in the surface states of the coating (see Fig. 1).

The crystal structure and the chemical composition were characterized by XRD and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peak which found at 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.1°, 55.8°/56.0°, 58.5°, 68.8°, 75.9°, 78.2°/78.3° and 87.5° observed on the patterns of each NBW sample. It corresponds sequentially to the (131), (200), (202), (331)/(133), (262), (400), (193), (460)/(402), and (462) crystal planes of Bi2WO6 (JCPDS NO.39-0256) (Jiang et al., 2022). In addition, no diffraction peak of N-CQDs was observed at 26°, mainly due to the low content and high dispersibility of N-CQDs. This also indicated that low crystallinity N-CQDs had no significant effect on the crystal structure of Bi2WO6. What's more, the solvent had little effect on the crystal form of NBW, indicating that ethylene glycol had no significant destructive effect on NBW (Thyda et al., 2022).

As shown the FT-IR in Fig. 2b, symmetric stretching vibrations of the O-W-O bond, asymmetric stretching vibrations of the W-O-W bond, and stretching vibration peaks of the W-O and Bi-O bonds appeared at 500-820 cm−1 in the spectra of BW and NBW (Liu et al., 2020c). Different from BW, the infrared spectrum of NBW showed a large number of indistinct chemical bonds containing N and C, which indicated that N-CQDs had been successfully introduced into BW. The infrared spectra of NBW and NBW-1-100 were consistent, and no new peak appeared, which means that no new chemical bond has been formed.

The XPS spectra of NBW were recorded in Fig. 2c–h. As shown in Fig. 2c, the elements contained in the material mainly included bismuth, tungsten, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen elements. Fig. 2d showed that the binding energy of Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2 were 159.2 eV and 164.4 eV respectively, which suggested that Bi mainly existed in the chemical state of Bi3+ (Kim and Jo, 2020). The Bi characteristic peaks near 157.7 eV and 163 eV were generally considered as characteristic peaks of Bi elemental (Hua et al., 2020). The high-resolution W 4f spectra of NBW and BW samples were shown in Fig. 2e. The binding energy of W 4f 5/2 and W 4f 7/2 were 36.9 eV and 34.7 eV respectively, which revealed that W mainly existed in the chemical state of W6+. In addition, the binding energy of W 4f decreased to a certain extent, which was attributed to the built-in Mott Schottky effect formed by the electron transfer between Bi metal and NBW (Zhang et al., 2018). The three peaks fitted near 284.5, 285.3, and 286.0 eV in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) was referred to as the presence of C-C/C=C, C-N, and C=O (Wang et al., 2023). In Fig. 2g, the peak of the N 1s spectrum was located at 400.1 eV, belonging to the N-H bond (Zhang et al., 2022). For the O1s spectra of NBW in Fig. 2h, the peaks at 529.5 eV and 532.1 were assigned to Bi-O and W-O bonds, respectively, while the peak at 533.6 eV was attributed to the adsorbed oxygen on the surface (Huo et al., 2019). Therefore, XPS analysis indicated that N-CQDs were closely bound to Bi2WO6, and through their combined action with ethylene glycol, and Bi metal was formed on the surface of NBW.

The microstructure and chemical composition of NBW were characterized by SEM. Fig. 3 a and b showed the SEM images of NBW, the shape of NBW was similar to a three-dimensional sphere with a diameter of 2 μm formed by the intersection and stacking of nanosheets, and a great quantity of nanosheet cluster structures appeared on the surface of the sphere. This structure allowed the catalyst to have a larger contact and adsorption area with pollutants, thus providing a more active site in the catalytic reaction (Yan et al., 2023). In the high-power scanning electron microscope images of NBW, the surface of NBW microspheres was covered with some materials within the range of 200–500 nm, which was attributed to fragmented nanosheets and bismuth metal reduced by ethylene glycol. SEM hardly observed N-CQDs, mainly due to their small size and uniform dispersion. From the EDS element mapping (Fig. 3c), Bi, W, C, N, and O were evenly distributed in the NBW hybrid material, whcih was consistent with the results of XPS.

The elemental spectra of energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) were used to analyze the elemental content of different sites in NBW (Fig. 3d and e). The results showed that the element content of Bi in the surface attachment micro area of NBW significantly increased compared to the relatively smooth area, while the content of W and O did not follow this pattern, indicating that the main component of the attachment was bismuth element (Zhang et al., 2019). The fine structure of NBW was further revealed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 3f and g. There were numerous small black dots with a diameter of about 5 nm scattered on the surface and inside of the spherical material NBW. N-CQDs with different degrees of aggregation were successfully tightly bound to Bi2WO6 (Li et al., 2023). It was worth noting that light colored microspheres with a diameter of about 10 nm widely present at the edge of NBW were considered as Bi metals on the surface of NBW materials (Wang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2022a). The stripes of different materials in HRTEM also confirmed the distribution of N-CQDs and Bi on NBW, which was consistent with the conclusions of XPS and EDX.

As shown the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy in Fig. 4 a, the introduction of N-CQDs was beneficial for BW to absorb light at wavelengths of 200–800 nm, which was consistent with the absorption edge redshift phenomenon. It showed that the hybrid material had good optical absorption characteristics. This was mainly because N-CQDs was used as the optical center to absorb visible light (Wang et al., 2023). In addition, the interface charge transfer effect, the small size effect of N-CQDs, and the quantum confinement effect jointly promoted the formation of electron-hole pairs, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity. In addition, compared to NBW-1-100, the introduction of Bi metal was conducive to enhanced light absorption after 500 nm, which was attributed to the charge transfer characteristics of Bi and BW materials, effectively inhibited the recombination of electron holes by adsorbing electrons (Chen et al., 2021). By using the Kubelka-Munk equation to calculate the bandgap, it was shown that under the coupling effect of multiple effects, the bandgap value of NBW was significantly reduced (Fig. 4b). In addition, the results of photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy also confirmed the above results (Fig. 4c). Due to the conjugated p-structure of N-CQDs and the band bending caused by carbonyl and carboxyl groups, the recombination rate of electrons and holes in BW was significantly reduced, indicating that N-CQDs effectively inhibited electron-hole recombination (Liu et al., 2020a). Excessive N-CQDs tended to aggregate into large particles (>10 nm), serving as new recombination centers for electron holes. Therefore, there was an optimal dose for N-CQDs.

The transient photocurrent response curves of BW and NBW were measured as shown in Fig. 4d, and the photocurrent intensity of NBW material was higher than that of BW. Therefore, the lifetime of electron-hole pairs in NBW was longer than that of BW. This was attributed not only to CQDs, but also to the fact that Bi metals helped transfer electrons to suppress electron-hole recombination (Ding et al., 2023). In addition, N doping effectively induced charge delocalization and reduced the work function of CQDs (Liu et al., 2023). All samples only showed one semicircle in the EIS spectrum (Fig. 4e), indicating that the photocatalytic reaction only designs surface charge transfer steps. The radiation of NBW and BW under light was lower than that under dark conditions, indicating that light excitation generated more charge carriers. In addition, the radius of NBW was smaller than that of BW, indicating that NBW had stronger charge transfer ability and lower reaction resistance at the semiconductor interface. The easier separation of carriers and lower electron hole recombination rate in NBW-1 often represent longer carrier lifetimes, which was consistent with the results of PL (Nithya et al., 2014).

Fig. 4f compared the N2 adsorption/desorption curves and pore size distribution of different BW catalysts. The isotherm of photocatalysts was all type IV, with an H3 hysteresis loop, which indicated the presence of mesopores (type H3), corresponding to slit-shaped pores, typically associated with plate-like particles, which corresponded to their sheet-like morphology. The isotherm of the samples almost coincided, indicating that their structures were similar. The introduction of small-sized quantum dots and the generation of vacancies increased the specific surface area of photocatalysts in Table 1 (Hojamberdiev et al., 2014). However, excessive quantum dots did not consistently favor the specific surface area of the catalyst due to their aggregation. In addition, BET surface area and pore structure were not the most critical factors affecting the photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6 microspheres, as the differences among samples were not significant.

The ability of NBW samples to remove NO under full spectrum and visible light irradiation was used to evaluate their photocatalytic activity. Compared with BW, the photocatalytic ability of NBW-1 had been greatly improved, reaching 52% under visible light irradiation and 64% under corresponding full spectrum irradiation in Fig. 5 a and b. Later, increasing the amount of N-CQDs reduced the oxidation efficiency of NO by NBW, because the excessive N-CQDs covered the active site of BW and formed competitive adsorption of photons with BW, thus affecting the photocatalytic activity which was consistent with the conclusions in Fig. 4. In Fig. 5c, potassium iodide (KI), sodium oxalate (SO), tert butanol (IPA), and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) were used to trap the holes (h+), electrons (e−), hydroxyl radical (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2 −) of the catalyst in turn (Ma et al., 2022). The results indicated that PBQ and KI significantly inhibited the catalytic activity of the catalyst. Therefore, •O2 − and h+ played a major role in NO conversion (Lin et al., 2023).

The oxidation ability of different solvent systems NBW for NO was compared in Fig. 5d. Based on the conclusion of Fig. 4, NBW-1-50 had better photocatalytic activity due to the Bi metal. In addition, the poor activity of NBW-1-0 might be due to the poor dispersion of sodium tungstate in ethylene glycol, resulting in insufficient reaction. In addition, the reaction conditions during the BW synthesis stage were optimized in Fig. 5e and f. At a hydrothermal temperature of 200 °C, the reaction rate was too fast, leading to excessive grain size and possible breeding of by-products, resulting in impure products. However, 160 °C caused the reaction to be incomplete, thereby small grain size. Similarly, hydrothermal time also affects the photocatalytic activity of NBW (Song, 2018). The NBW prepared by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 20 h exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity when the volume ratio of water to ethylene glycol was 1 (Jaruwat et al., 2018).

In fact, the durability of photocatalytic stabilizers was crucial for their practical applications. As shown in Fig. 6 , under visible light/full spectrum irradiation, the oxidation efficiency of NO by NBW slightly decreased after five consecutive uses, which was collected and cleaned with ethanol/water solvent, then replenished it with fresh NBW to 0.1g, the photocatalytic activity was restored to a certain extent (Fig. 6a and b). FT-IR spectra and XRD patterns of NBW before and after cycle tests showed that the conversion of NO did not significantly affect the crystal phase and chemical bonds of NBW (Fig. 6c and d). This was in line with its repeatable photocatalytic activity. The above indicated that NBW had long-term photostability activity and durability.

In order to disperse NBW evenly in the coating system and maintain the self-cleaning performance of the coating, PFDTES and TEOS were used to modify NBW, denoted as F-NBW. The modified F-NBW exhibited characteristic peaks of -CF3 and Si-O compared to NBW in FT-IR, indicating that NBW had been successfully modified (Fig. 7 a). As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorbance at 450 nm and state changes of NBW and F-NBW in ethyl acetate suspension was compared to determine the dispersion and stability of CPFB precursor. The absorbance values of both solutions decreased with the prolongation of standing time, while the absorbance values of F-NBW decreased relatively slowly, indicating that F-NBW had better dispersibility in ethyl acetate. At 30 min, the absorbance value of F-NBW was still much higher than that of NBW, and there was also a significant delay in the stratification phenomenon. Fluorination modification significantly improved the dispersibility and stability of NBW in ethyl acetate. In addition, UV–vis spectroscopy analysis was conducted on the coating mixture in Fig. 7c. Unlike coating CPF without catalyst and coating with BW added, NBW-1 endowed the coating with high visible absorption strength, mainly due to the stability and dispersibility of F-NBW with high intrinsic light absorption strength within CPFB (Guo et al., 2017; Saeed et al., 2016).

The thermal stability of CPFB was studied using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) in Fig. 7d. No adsorbed water was observed inside the coating and the coating lost weight rapidly at 400–600 °C. This was mainly attributed to the decomposition of some fluorinated groups in PTFE, PVDF, and F-NBW inside the coating (DeLisio et al., 2016). The highly rough surface of CPFB was composed of NBW microspheres, curled PTFE, and PVDF, with components of different structures interwoven to form a cross-linked network (Fig. 7e and f). Coral-shaped PVDF/PTFE and NBW microsphere structures were clearly observed in high-power SEM images. Rough hierarchical structure often endowed the coating with better self-cleaning performance and a large number of photocatalytic active site (Jadhavar et al., 2020).

NO conversion experiments of CPFB were conducted on the cement plates under visible/full spectrum irradiation conditions. The degradation ability of the coating to NO gradually increased with the increase of NBW content. The oxidation rate of CPFB-2 under visible light was 74%, while the removal rate under full spectrum was 87% (Fig. 8 a and b). However, the excessive catalyst of CPFB-3 caused self aggregation, resulting in the mutual covering of active sites, which in turn reduced catalytic activity. Ethyl acetate reagent dispersed with quantitative capture agent was sprayed on the surface of CPFB-2 to test its photocatalytic ability. Similar to the results of the catalyst, the photocatalytic activity of CPFB-2 after PBQ and KI interference was almost completely inhibited (Fig. 8c). Therefore, •O2 − and h+ played a major role in the conversion of NO.

The surface of coating was sprayed with 10 mg/L methyl red dye and treated with xenon lamp illumination. The surface coloring of coatings was recorded to simulate the effect of photocatalysis on methyl red dye. As the NBW content increased, the change in color difference gradually increased. After 36 h, CPFB-3 almost returned to an uncontaminated state. while CPF remained deeply colored. In addition, after being contaminated with methyl red and illuminated to full spectrum for 36 h, CPFB-2 was subjected to NO oxidation testing under visible/full spectrum irradiation. The results showed that 55% NO has been removed under visible light and 72% NO under the irradiation of full spectrum (Fig. 8d).

For clear quantitative comparison, the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model (L-H) was used to fit the curve of photocatalytic removal of NO [Xia et al., 2019b]. Obviously, the initial photocatalytic removal of NO is believed to follow first-order kinetics, as shown in the linear plot of ln (C/C0) versus time (t) in Fig. 8e. Under visible or full spectrum illumination, the normalized NO removal rate of CPFB-2 is significantly higher than that of NBW-0.5 Therefore, it is inferred that the feasibility of loading NBW-0.5 onto CPFB-2 to remove NO.

The NO cycling oxidation experiment of CPFB-2 was shown in Fig. 9 a and b. During continuous operation, catalytic products accumulated on the surface of CPFB-2, resulting in the coverage of catalytic active sites and decrease in photocatalytic activity. However, the PCD efficiency of the CPFB-2 was greatly improved after cleaning, indicating that CPFB-2 had good stability and recyclability. In Fig. 9c, the FT-IR spectra of CPFB-2 after five cycles of use and rinsing were compared with the fresh coating, and no significant functional group differences were observed, verifying the stability and feasibility of self-cleaning CPFB-2 (Fig. 9c).

The electron transfer characteristics of coatings with different NBW doping levels were evaluated using EIS and the transient photocurrent response curve of CPFB. The transfer resistance of CPFB was negatively correlated with the doping amount of NBW, while its transient photocurrent was positively correlated with the doping amount of NBW (Fig. 9d and e). The PL spectrum used to characterize the lifetime of electron-hole pairs also confirmed the regularity of electron-hole recombination rate in CPFB powder, which was consistent with previous experimental results (Fig. 9f).

The hydrophobicity and durability of coatings were very important in practical applications. Therefore, the hydrophobicity of coatings under different NBW dosages was discussed (Fig. 10 a). CPFB-2 showed a CA greater than 150° for water and glycerol, which was greater than 140° for highly polar (ethylene glycol). 0.2 g NBW fully combined with PFDTES, which greatly reduced the surface energy of CPFB-2. As a polymer material, PVDF not only provided a positive effect on reducing the surface energy of the coating, but also worked together with PTFE and an appropriate amount of NBW to construct a complex rough surface structure. These two reasons promoted the non wetting of liquids on the material surface. However, excessive NBW couldn't continuously improve the hydrophobicity of the coating, mainly because the excess NBW didn't participate in low surface energy modification. And a large amount of spherical NBW accumulated and damaged the rough structure of the coating surface.

Seven aqueous solutions with pH values of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13 were prepared using NaOH and H2SO4 solutions, respectively, which were dyed with rhodamine and methylene blue. As shown in Fig. 10b and c, the water CA of all droplets on the CPFB-2 surface was greater than 150°, and the droplets was quasi-spherical suggesting that CPFB-2 had good non-wetting ability to aqueous solutions. In addition, the sliding angle of water droplets with different pH values on the surface of CPFB-2 was less than 10°, which indirectly demonstrated their superhydrophobic properties.

The durability of the coating was a very important indicator during its use, the CA and photocatalytic activity of CPFB-2 were measured after photo oxidation, water impact, wear resistance, and chemical corrosion. After 48 h of direct irradiation with a 500 W xenon lamp, CPFB-2 still maintained superhydrophobicity (Fig. 11 a). The CA of water droplets on the surface of CPFB-2 was greater than 150°, while the CA of ethylene glycol and glycerol on the surface of CPFB-2 was greater than 135°. This was mainly due to the photo oxidation resistance of PTFE and PVDF, a small amount of TEOS also played certain protective role. In Fig. 11b, which simulated the process of CPFB-2 being rinsed during daily use, CPFB-2 generally maintained superhydrophobic properties after six cycles, while the non-wetting properties of ethylene glycol and glycerol on the surface of CPFB-2 were also reduced. Therefore, CPFB-2 had liquid repellency. In addition, after 25 wear experiments, some obvious wear marks appeared on the surfaces of CPFB-2, and the CA of water droplets gradually decreased to 149° (Fig. 11c). This was mainly because of the influence of friction on the graded structure of rough surfaces of CPFB-2. Fortunately, CPFB-2 still retained hydrophobicity, mainly due to the remaining rough structure and low surface energy materials still functioning.

The results of CPFB-2 corrosion resistance experiment were shown in Fig. 11d. After continuous immersion for 24 h, the CA of the liquid on CPFB-2 surface decreased to some extent. Among them, acid immersion had a significant impact on CPFB-2. Based on the alkaline modification environment of NBW-1, it was speculated that the acidic conditions were responsible for the destruction of the low surface energy F-NBW structure. Fortunately, the CPFB-2 surface still maintained high hydrophobicity, mainly due to the high chemical corrosion resistance of PTFE and PVDF, and the rough structure of CPFB-2 was not significantly damaged. In addition, the PCD test of CPFB-2 after the durability test was completed, which showed that there was a significant loss of the NO degradation ability of CPFB-2 under visible light, compared to the smaller decrease in the NO degradation ability of CPFB-2 under full spectrum (Fig. 11e and f), which was mainly due to a certain loss of N-CQDs. However, CPFB-2 still retained catalytic activity for NO removal, which also verified the feasibility of CPFB-2 for NO degradation.

Several powders with different particle sizes were selected to test the removal ability of CPFB-2 on surface powder pollutants (Liu et al., 2021a,b,c). The results indicated that water dripped on the surface of CPFB-2 and carried away pollutants. In addition, this process also carried away the nitrate generated during the photocatalytic process, ensuring the long-term usability of CPFB-2 (Fig. 12 a). In addition, the anti-dust adhesion of CPFB-2 was tested (Fig. 12b). The results indicated that the smaller the particle size, the more dust adheres to the CPFB-2 surface, and the rough structure of the CPFB-2 surface seemed to tend to adsorb the adhesive. However, under the impact of water, the attachments on the CPFB-2 surface were easily detached. This indicated that CPFB-2 had good self-cleaning performance.

The rhodamine and methylene blue powder attached to CPFB-2 were washed (Fig. 12c). After the water flow carried the dye powder away from the CPFB-2 surface, the CPFB-2 surface was not severely polluted. CPFB-2 was vertically immersed in the aqueous solution of Rhodamine and methylene blue (Fig. 12d). There was no difference in the coloring of CPFB before and after impregnation, which verified that CPFB-2 had excellent hydrophobicity and resistance to organic dye adhesion.

Based on the above research and previous studies (Liu et al., 2021a,b,c), the synergistic mechanism of liquid repellency and photocatalysis in CPFB-2 was revealed (Fig. 13 ). The photocatalytic performance of CPFB-2 was mainly provided by the NBW-1 sphere on the surface. Due to the Bi and the N-CQDs, NBW-1 spheres had more electron transfer centers and stronger light absorption ability, greatly improving the photocatalytic activity of NBW-1. The general process of degradation was that under light conditions, NBW-1 generated many reducing electrons and oxidizing holes, which converted O2 into superoxide radicals (•O2 −) with strong oxidation ability. In addition, a very small number of water molecules on the surface were subjected to h+ interactions to generate •OH, which had a certain positive effect on NO oxidation. The NO adsorbed on the material surface was oxidized to nitrate by •O2 −, •OH, and h+. The NO adsorbed on the material surface was oxidized to nitrate by •O2 −, •OH, and h+ (Liu et al., 2021a,b,c).

In addition, if NBW-1 was directly coated on the surface of the test board, the catalyst was easily contaminated. The mixture of dust, organic pollutants, degradation products, and water were wrapped on the surface of NBW-1, which greatly reduced the adsorption of pollutants on NBW-1. If most of the active sites of NBW-1 were covered, photocatalytic activity was also greatly inhibited. The organic pollutants on the surface of CPFB-2 with the synergistic effect of self-cleaning and photocatalysis were effectively degraded into small molecule products. The pollutants adhering to the surface of CPFB-2 were also easily completely removed by the rolling action of water, making it easy to clean and maintain the photocatalytic activity of CPFB-2 for a long time. This article proposed a bismuth based self-cleaning photocatalytic coating that responded to visible light. NBW-1 with SPR effect provided the photocatalytic performance of CPFB-2, with NO oxidation efficiency of 74% and 87% under visible light and full spectrum, respectively, and effectively degraded methyl red. The water CA of CPFB-2 exceeded 150°, and it also had non wetting properties towards polar liquids. CPFB-2 still exhibited hydrophobicity and photocatalytic activity after 48 h of photo oxidation, 6 spray gun cleaning, 25 sandpaper abrasion, and 24 h of acid/alkali/salt immersion. Meanwhile, the resistance of CPFB-2 to pollutants and organic dye adhesion, as well as its self-cleaning ability, had also been confirmed. In summary, the feasibility of this self-cleaning photocatalytic coating provided a certain reference for the design and practical application of visible light responsive photocatalytic coatings in the future. Based on the potential application of CPFB-2 for building exterior walls and tunnel interior walls, future work can consider improving the mechanical strength and oil resistance of self-cleaning coatings.
